title: The Macro Minute: Why haven’t stocks crashed yet?

Happy Thursday out there at 10:42, it's
your skipper here, Darius Della Penna,
in our macro minute for Thursday, March
26, 2026. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
why haven't stocks crashed yet?
Our answer is because the Bayesian
priors of the growth, inflation,
monetary policy, fiscal policy, and
liquidity cycles were all generally
positive for most institutional
investors at the start of this conflict.
There hasn't been enough time and
reported macro and micro data to cause
them to revise their GDP and earnings
expectations materially lower.
These negative revisions are forthcoming
and will snowball as long as the Iran
conflict removes petroleum products from
the global economy.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Starting with
the growth cycle. The latest initial and
continuing jobless claims data currently
signal a low probability of a developing
recession in the US economy. The 42
macro five-five recession signaling
indicators currently signal a low
probability of a developing recession in
the US economy as well, as does the 42
macro business cycle model. As we've
discussed with our global investor
community since the outset of this
conflict, cross-asset crashes occur when
our positioning model signals the
crowded bullish positioning and when new
information causes investors to
materially and durably question the
generally positive Bayesian priors
signaled by our macro weather model
regarding the other five key macro
cycles.
So, let me say that again.
Cross-asset crashes occur when our
positioning model signals a store
signals a crowded bullish positioning,
check, and when new information causes
investors to materially and durably
question the generally positive Bayesian
prior signaled by our macro weather
model regarding the other five key macro
cycles, check.
Said simply, markets crash when
consensus is crowded on the bullish side
of the trade and surprise and surprising
economic or policy developments force
them to abandon their positive
expectations. The risk of this
behavioral dynamic occurring over the
next month or two is extremely high from
our perspective given the precondition
of a historic degree of crowded bullish
positioning heading into the year,
everyone thinking growth, inflation,
monetary policy, fiscal policy, and
liquidity all being generally positive.
Not Sorry, not everyone. Institutional
investors, the folks we're meeting with
in our pro-to-pro meetings.
The YouTube community is always bearish
and they the perma bears get even
louder.
The
higher the PE multiple on the S&P goes.
So, that's not that's not a real
legitimate process, but the people with
real legitimate processes managing
hundreds of billions and trillions of
dollars across global financial markets,
the kinds of folks we communicate with
on a regular basis in our pro-to-pro
service, they generally think things are
fine. And so, the longer this goes on,
coming from a starting point of historic
degree of crowded bullish positioning,
the more likely it is that markets start
to spiral to the downside alongside GDP
and earnings estimates. That is
forthcoming every day on the x-axis that
this crisis persists. Investors need to
be aware of that. Uh with regards to the
inflation cycle, amid ongoing indirect
negotiations taking place via Pakistan,
the Pentagon is evaluating escalatory
scenarios featuring both ground troop
deployment and large-scale aerial
assault framed as a quote final blow.
Uh I think the

state-owned press indicates Tehran is
demanding explicit guarantees that

operations as a prerequisite for
de-escalation. Additional demands
include financial reparations for
wartime damages and formal
acknowledgement of Tehran's sovereignty
over the Strait of Hormuz.
It's exactly what we've been warning
about all week. Uh actually going back
to last week. Iranian lawmakers are also
drafting legislation to institutionalize
a transit fee for vessels navigating the
Strait of Hormuz, effectively monetizing
maritime security provision along this
critical choke point for where which,
you know, 20 At the start of the
conflict, roughly 20% of the global
crude oil was traveling through there.
Elsewhere, in Japan, February PPI data
supported outlook for accelerating
inflation. Uh nothing actionable with
respect to our Bayesian priors for the
monetary policy, fiscal policy, and
liquidity cycles. And finally, with
respect to the positioning cycle, none
of the aforementioned Iranian demands
are included in Washington's 15-point
framework proposal delivered to the
Islamic Republic earlier this week.
Again, none of those demands were part
of Trump's 15-point framework that got
markets to rally earlier this week. Uh
it is unclear whether Israel will agree
to any deal struck between the US and
Iran given the objectives and likely
significant damage to Israeli
infrastructure from Iran's persistent
missile barrages. Moreover, regional
powers including Saudi Arabia and the
UAE are weighing potential military
involvement against Iran contingent upon
Iran targeting critical energy or water
infrastructure, which sources
characterize as a high bar for
escalation.
UAE Ambassador to the US, Yousef Al
Otaiba, wrote in the Wall Street Journal
op-ed, "We can't let Iran hold the US,
the United Arab Emirates Emirates, and
the global economy hostage." Adding, "A
simple ceasefire isn't enough. We need a
conclusive outcome that addresses Iran's
full range of threats." Uh continue
reviewing and learn whether our
institutional grade risk management
overlays, KISS and Dr. Mo, are evolving
their signals in response to these or

then, so always we'll wrap up with a
question from our 42 macro community

clearly somebody knew something.

got greedy, pigs became hogs. I'm glad
we're already out of equities at that
moment, so thanks to DD for that. But my
question is, where does that leave us
as investors as investors if large
institutions have information advantages
and are using those advantages to
extract wealth from the market? Is it a
zero-sum game? If it is, these games are
actually coming at our expense and I
would like to know how we can remain
confident in markets in the kind of in
the face of this kind of activity. So,
um
Doing this for decades, I'll tell you
right now, someone always knows
something. That's step one.
The morally impaired will always use
MNPI, material non-public information,
to extract rents from global capital
markets. That's step two. And number
three, once you acknowledge the
the persistent, you know, the the the
durable persistence of step one and of
number one and number two, then you need
to take action as an investor to make
sure that you are you know, your
portfolio is safeguarded from that type
of activity. You can't do anything about
you know, people gaming the system. You
know, whether be the the the the Trump
family, the Kushner family, the Pelosi
family, you cannot do anything about
the, you know, the elites gaming the
system in this country or any country.
So, you have to just move on from
worrying about that or spending time on
Twitter complaining about that. Your
goal should be to retire on time and
comfortable. And you need to optimize
your investing habits and all the
choices that you make and particularly
putting guardrails around the choices
that you make because we all suffer from
cognitive biases that make us make
stupid decisions, particularly in weeks
like these. You need to optimize your
habits and your decisions towards
achieving your strategic investment
objective, which is retiring on time and
comfortable. And so, going back to this
question is, you know, what can we do as
retail investors? What can retail
investors do when you have this type of
activity ongoing in global financial
markets? Well, well, the step one is,
you want to make sure that the types of
decisions that you're making in your
portfolio are super high quality, the
kinds of decisions at the right time and
the right factors that institutional
investors, the folks who are closest to
the MNPI, are are making in their
portfolios. The folks who are extracting
the rents. You want to be, you know,
using tools like KISS and Dr. Mo, which
are quantitative, systematic,
institutional grade risk management
overlays to make sure that the choices
that you're making, whether it be from a
asset allocation or portfolio
construction perspective, are being made
and are very similar to to the types of
choices that the institutions who are
closer to all that MNPI are making in
their portfolios. You want to be
approaching financial market risk-taking
with the same kind of frameworks, the
same kind of models, and ultimately the
same kind of results of that these
institutions have. So, that's why 42
macro exists, is to eradicate that
asymmetry between what our buy-side
clients are doing out there from a risk
management standpoint and versus what
the median retail investor is doing out
there from a risk management standpoint.
There's a massive gap and that's why I
continue to warn investors retail
investors about taking factor risk in
financial markets. I'm not alone in that
warning. There have been many investors
who warned you guys about taking factor
risk in financial markets. Two of the
best investors of all time have
explicitly warned retail investors about
taking factor risk in financial markets,
including Warren Buffett and Ray Dalio.
This stuff is serious. There are people
with some way more sophisticated
systems, way more resources, way more
experience, way more academic
achievement, way more way higher EQ and
emotional intelligence in terms of their
ability to withstand drawdowns and and
not react to bull porn or bear porn.
They have all these advantages. And so,
my point is, if you're going to be
taking factor risk as a investor, you
need to first, before you make any
trades,
long, short, this, that, win, in, out,
before you make one trade taking factor
risk as a retail investor, you need to
honestly acknowledge all those
advantages on the other side of your
trade.
I'm pausing for dramatic effect. Before
you make a single trade as a retail
investor taking factor risk, you need to
open your mouth and say, "I acknowledge
that the retail the institutional
investors that Darius serves the his
protocol service at 42 macro are all
equipped with more resources in terms of
research and risk management signals.
They have better risk management
overlays that are backed by proven
quantitative techniques. Most of these
people have significant the advanced
academic achievement. They have higher
emotional intelligence in terms of their
ability to withstand drawdowns and
ignore bull porn and bear porn and just
make thoughtful dispassionate decisions
in financial markets. You need to say
all that stuff out loud before you make
one factor bet.
You need to acknowledge their advantage,
their structural advantages, which is
why this factor investing is a zero-sum
game. Alpha alpha generating in
financial markets, outperforming the
index, outperforming in financial
markets is a zero-sum game.
That's why there's so much churn and
turnover over the buy side because it's
really hard to consistently do that well
as Nobel Prize research from the likes
of Danny Kahneman and Amos Tversky and
Cass Sunstein and Phil Tetlock and
Richard Thaler and then all the other
folks at the Chicago U Chicago
uh you know factory would suggest you
know this this stuff's really hard. And
so the landing this plane
once you acknowledge all that stuff and
you recognize that hey you have a
structural advantage relative to all
those people competing in a very
difficult game and that's called the
ability to collect beta.
They can't get paid on collecting beta.
They have to generate returns above the
systematic return. They have to have
high information ratios, high Sharpe
ratios, high Sortino ratios, high Calmar
ratios.
If you don't understand what those
things are then you shouldn't be taking
factor risk in my opinion to begin with.
I would I would certainly not be taking
factor risk if you don't understand what
those things are.
And it's because again that's what
they're doing. That's that's that's the
game they're playing. And so if you want
to play their game, be be my guest, but
my goal and my goal is to help you
retire on time and comfortably. And the
best way to retire on time and
comfortably is not participate in that
game and go participate in the game
where it's rigged in your favor. And
that's called beta.
Go collect the beta. Go collect the mana
from the heavens when it's raining.
Close your basket and get in the tent
when it's not or sorry when the mana's
raining. Close your basket and get in
the tent when it's dark outside or when
it's storming. That's it. If you can do
that over and over again across market
cycles, which means you're generating a
positively skewed return distribution in
your account, which no retail investor
can do without the use of sophisticated
institutional risk management systems,
you create a positively skewed return
distribution in your account
and compound returns from a higher net
asset value so that compounding works
and grows faster exponentially,
you will make more money across multiple
market cycles. You will make more money
faster and ultimately achieve your
strategic investment objective, which is
you know, retiring on time and
comfortably. At least it should be
retiring on time and comfortably. So
just wrapping up, the key takeaway is
you don't have to participate in the
game where you're the it's the it's
rigged against you. You should
participate in the game where it's where
it's rigged in your favor, which is what
KISS is designed to do.
And if you insist on participating in
the game where it's rigged against you,
then you definitely need institutional
grade risk management overlays to make
sure that the decisions and and and
habits that you are uh bringing to the
table are at least as closely aligned to
the decisions and the habits that
they're bringing to the table on the
other side of your trades. And that's
why Dr. Mo uh exists. Um so uh hopefully
that was helpful. Uh again, the system's
rigged. You know it's rigged. We know
it's rigged. Stop complaining about it
being rigged and go do something about
